title: Pepperoncini White Bean Stew
intro:
Pepperoncini White Bean Stew

Pepperoncini braises became popular in American home kitchens because the peppers and their brine deliver salt, acidity, and gentle heat in one ingredient. Many versions rely on fatty cuts of beef cooked slowly in a crockpot. This stew keeps the same garlic and pepperoncini backbone but builds the body with mushrooms, white beans, and pearled barley. The result is hearty, rustic, and bright instead of heavy, with a broth designed to be soaked up with hot crusty bread.

items: Soak the dried white beans overnight in plenty of water. Drain, cover with fresh water, and simmer about 60–90 minutes until tender. Drain and set aside.
Heat a large enameled cast iron Dutch oven on the induction range over medium heat. Add the olive oil.
Add the diced onion and cook about 5 minutes, stirring occasionally, until softened and lightly golden.
Add the whole garlic cloves and cook 3–4 minutes, stirring occasionally, until lightly browned in spots.

Add the thick-sliced mushrooms, kosher salt, and black pepper. Let them cook undisturbed for about 3–4 minutes so they release moisture and begin browning. Stir and continue cooking another 5–6 minutes until most of the moisture evaporates and the mushrooms develop good color.
Add the dry pearled barley and cook about 2 minutes, stirring frequently to lightly toast the grains.
Add the pepperoncini peppers, ½ cup of the pepperoncini brine, the beef stock, rosemary sprig, and chopped oregano. Scrape the bottom of the pot firmly with a spoon to release any browned bits.
Bring the stew to a gentle simmer. Reduce heat to low and cook uncovered for about 25 minutes so the barley begins to soften and hydrate.
Stir in the cooked white beans and continue simmering about 20 minutes more, until the barley is tender and the broth thickens slightly and the garlic cloves have become soft.
Stir in the remaining ½ cup pepperoncini brine and simmer another 5 minutes.
Press several of the softened garlic cloves against the side of the pot with the back of a spoon so they dissolve slightly into the broth and give it a naturally creamy body.
Remove the rosemary sprig and stir in the chopped parsley.
Serve in wide bowls with thick slices of hot crusty bread. Spread the softened garlic cloves onto the bread and use it to soak up the pepperoncini broth.
